text:
Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation through Aqueous-Phase Chemistry of Biomass Burning Phenols The large number of biomass burning (BB) emission components that have yet to be individually studied for secondary organic aerosol (SOA) precursor potential motivate research into how BB emissions as a whole contribute to atmospheric SOA and its associated chemical and physical impacts. The photo-oxidation of several biomass burning phenolic compounds present in BB BB emission components were studied in aqueous solution to elucidate the pathways and mechanisms of aerosol formation of ambient biomass burning derived secondary organic aerosol (SOA) via aqueous-phase processing. The oxidation was carried out with two reactive species, namely the triplet excited state of an aromatic carbonyl ( ^3C^* ) and the hydroxyl radical (•OH). An in-depth and multifaceted characterization of aqueous SOA (aqSOA) was performed here using aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS), nanospray desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (nano-DESI MS) and ion chromatography (IC). The outcome was the production of a highly diverse and complex solution containing a plethora of low volatility products including functionalised monomers and acids as well as a diverse series of oligomers ranging from dimers up to hexamers.

The resulting phenolic aqSOA was characterized by high levels of oxygenation, average O/C ratios ranging from 0.85 to 1.23, which are comparable to ambient LV-OOA. Although the molecular composition was similar in both cases, $^3$C$^*$ reactions proceeded faster, producing more oligomers and hydroxylated products than those resulting from $\cdot$OH reactions at the same extent of precursor consumption. Thermodenuder measurements indicate that the stability of the aqSOA was determined by both the level of oxygenation and its oligomeric structure. The resulting aqSOA exhibits strong light absorption in the UV–visible range, indicating that aqueous phenol oxidation in clouds may be an important secondary source of brown carbon.
explanation: The text uses authentic-sounding aerosol chemistry terminology and typical manuscript structure. A small odd repetition and formatting quirks could hint at AI generation, but the content remains highly plausible for a human author.
